title: Kundavai, the Chola Princess
subtitle: The Gentle Hand Behind the Tiger Throne
moral: Power used to heal and build outlasts power used only to conquer.

sources.citation: Chola inscriptions recording Kundavai's endowments (temples and the atula-salai hospital)
K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, The CōĻas

panels.shot: majestic three-quarter low-angle of a royal woman standing between a temple vimana and a hospital hall

dialogue.text: In the age of Rajaraja Chola, Kundavai Pirati wove power into temples, healing houses and hope.

panels.shot: wide establishing shot from slightly elevated angle

dialogue.text: In the rich Kaveri valley, where canals shone like silver threads, the Chola war-banner rose over crowded markets and ancient shrines.
Born to princes and kings, Kundavai grew in halls of stone and sound, taught scriptures, accounts and the duties of rule.
While princes trained with spear and horse, the princess studied granaries and gifts, learning how gold moved, how hunger rose and fell.
As her younger brother Arulmolivarman rose as Rajaraja, inscriptions would name her Pirati, honoured above queens in the Chola courts.
Her name spread in stone. In temple after temple, records spoke of Kundavai’s lands, jewels and tax-shares, set aside for lamp, flower and food.
These gifts were not mere piety. They fed musicians, dancers, guards and cooks, binding town and temple into one living body.

panels.shot: medium shot with depth of field

dialogue.text: “The gods need lamps,” she murmurs, “but people need more.”
So the inscriptions speak of an atula-salai, a hospital endowed by Kundavai, raised beside sacred waters.
Here, the sick lay on mats, the poor and pregnant found shelter, and the state’s wealth turned into cool hands and bitter cures.

panels.shot: close-up on face in three-quarter profile

dialogue.text: “War passes,” she thinks, “but these stones will speak.”

panels.shot: medium shot, interior with musicians in background

dialogue.text: Her grants named singers, dancers, drum-beaters and readers, securing the pulse of art within the stone ribs of the temples.

panels.shot: wide hall shot from behind her shoulder

dialogue.text: She guided gifts like water, choosing which village, which canal, which shrine, so that each grant tied the countryside tighter to the crown.

panels.shot: medium shot in a quieter inner chamber

dialogue.text: “Remember,” she says softly, “charity must have order.”
She saw campaigns march out and triumphal processions return, but her work lay in what remained when drums fell silent.

panels.shot: high-angle view of an inscription being carved

dialogue.text: On walls of Rajaraja’s temples and in lesser shrines, the records fixed her choices, setting rules for how each coin must serve.

panels.shot: time-lapse style wide shot concept, static image showing different eras

dialogue.text: Centuries would pass. Kingdoms would shift. Yet lamps still burned, voices still rose, and healers worked in the houses she endowed.
Thus history remembers Kundavai not by battles, but by temples lit, arts nourished and the quiet reach of a princess who turned power toward care.
